questions.text: Which of the following are limitations of the hanging drop test for verifying Veress needle placement? Select all that apply.

answerOptions.text: A) Susceptible to false positives in obese patients
B) Relies on patient position and abdominal tone
C) Cannot distinguish between preperitoneal and intraperitoneal placement
D) Has lower sensitivity than intraabdominal pressure monitoring
E) Requires insufflation to perform correctly

questions.explanation: The hanging drop test is unreliable—affected by obesity and abdominal tone, prone to false positives, and cannot reliably differentiate preperitoneal from intraperitoneal placement. Intraabdominal pressure monitoring is more sensitive.

questions.text: Which of the following statements about laparoscopic entry techniques are incorrect? Select all that are incorrect.

answerOptions.text: A) Direct trocar entry requires prior insufflation
B) Optical trocars allow visualization during tissue penetration
C) Open (Hassan) technique provides tactile feedback but not direct vision
D) No single technique is clearly superior in preventing bowel injury
E) Surgeon experience influences complication rates

questions.explanation: Direct trocar entry does not need prior insufflation. Hasson/open technique DOES provide direct visualization. Other statements are correct.

questions.text: Which 3 factors most strongly support the use of Palmer’s Point for laparoscopic entry instead of umbilical access? Select 3 best answers.

answerOptions.text: A) Prior midline laparotomy with dense adhesions
B) BMI <20 with a thin abdominal wall
C) Prior umbilical hernia mesh repair
D) Right upper quadrant pain and guarding
E) Enlarged fibroid uterus reaching the umbilicus

questions.explanation: Midline laparotomy → adhesions behind umbilicus; prior umbilical mesh repair distorts anatomy; a large fibroid uterus can obstruct/alter umbilical entry—Palmer’s Point is safer in these settings.

questions.text: Which 2 intraoperative signs most suggest that the Veress needle was placed into the preperitoneal space rather than the peritoneal cavity? Select 2 best answers.

answerOptions.text: A) Low initial insufflation pressure (<10 mmHg)
B) High insufflation pressure (>15 mmHg) with minimal abdominal distension
C) Free flow of saline through the Veress needle
D) Poor visualization despite expected insufflation volume
E) Sudden drop in end-tidal CO₂

questions.explanation: Preperitoneal placement gives high pressure with poor distension and poor visualization despite volume. Low initial pressure is reassuring; ETCO₂ drop suggests gas embolism.

questions.text: Which of the following increase the risk of trocar-site hernia formation? Select all that apply.

answerOptions.text: A) Extraction through midline 10 mm dilating trocar
B) Use of 12 mm cutting trocar in LLQ
C) Repeated insertion and removal of trocar
D) Fascial closure of 5 mm port
E) Specimen extraction via transverse suprapubic incision

questions.explanation: Midline extraction, larger cutting trocars, and repeated manipulation enlarge/strain fascial defects; 5 mm closure is usually unnecessary; suprapubic transverse sites have lower hernia risk.

questions.text: Which 2 anatomic landmarks best predict safe lateral port placement? Select 2 best answers.

answerOptions.text: A) Medial umbilical ligament
B) Inferior epigastric vessels under transillumination
C) Round ligament insertion
D) Iliac crest height
E) ASIS and the course of iliohypogastric nerve

questions.explanation: Identify and avoid the inferior epigastrics (often visible with transillumination) and respect lateral abdominal wall nerve paths near the ASIS (iliohypogastric/ilioinguinal).
questions.submodule: Module 6.6 – Safety and Lateral Port Placement

questions.text: Which physical test is most sensitive for confirming successful Veress needle placement?

answerOptions.text: A) Hanging drop test
B) Saline aspiration test
C) Double click test
D) Intraabdominal pressure <10 mmHg

questions.explanation: An initial insufflation pressure <10 mmHg best correlates with correct intraperitoneal placement.

questions.text: When using the double click test at the umbilicus, the clicks correspond to penetration through which structures?

answerOptions.text: A) Rectus muscle and bladder
B) Fascia and peritoneum
C) Subcutaneous fat and fascia
D) Peritoneum and bowel

questions.explanation: At the umbilicus, two tactile/audible ‘clicks’ are felt—through fascia and then peritoneum.

questions.text: Why is the hanging drop test considered less reliable for verifying intraperitoneal placement?

answerOptions.text: A) It only detects blood
B) It is not useful in thin patients
C) It has low sensitivity and can give false reassurance
D) It requires carbon dioxide insufflation first

questions.explanation: Low sensitivity and confounders (e.g., tone/obesity) make it unreliable as a standalone test.

questions.text: What is the most common site for laparoscopic entry in gynecologic surgery?

answerOptions.text: A) Palmer’s Point
B) Right lower quadrant
C) Umbilicus
D) Posterior cul-de-sac

questions.explanation: The umbilicus is standard due to thin fascia and favorable anatomy.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is true regarding entry techniques?

answerOptions.text: A) Hassan technique cannot be used in obese patients
B) Veress needle is safer than optical trocar in all cases
C) Direct trocar entry is always contraindicated
D) There is no evidence that one entry technique is superior to others

questions.explanation: Systematic reviews don’t show clear superiority; choice depends on patient factors and surgeon experience.

questions.text: What is the primary benefit of the Hassan technique?

answerOptions.text: A) It avoids need for fascial closure
B) It provides direct visualization of tissue layers
C) It guarantees faster access
D) It avoids use of general anesthesia

questions.explanation: Open (Hasson) technique allows direct vision during fascia/peritoneum entry.

questions.text: Where is Palmer’s Point located?

answerOptions.text: A) 2 cm above the umbilicus, midline
B) Left midclavicular line, 3 cm below the costal margin
C) Right lower quadrant, 4 cm from ASIS
D) Posterior vaginal fornix

questions.explanation: Classic Palmer’s Point location minimizes risk to underlying structures.

questions.text: Which of the following is a contraindication to Palmer’s Point entry?

answerOptions.text: A) Obesity
B) Large pelvic mass
C) History of gastric bypass
D) Prior cesarean delivery

questions.explanation: Prior upper abdominal surgery raises adhesion/injury risk at Palmer’s Point.

questions.text: Why is the posterior vaginal fornix not commonly used for laparoscopic insufflation?

answerOptions.text: A) It's not effective at insufflation
B) It carries high risk of peritonitis
C) It is contraindicated in cul-de-sac obliteration or masses
D) It leads to poor visualization

questions.explanation: Cul-de-sac disease or masses make posterior fornix entry unsafe.

questions.text: What percentage of laparoscopic injuries occur during primary entry?

answerOptions.text: B) About 10%
C) One-third
D) None

questions.explanation: A substantial portion—around one-third—occurs during initial access.

questions.text: What is the estimated risk of encountering umbilical adhesions after a vertical midline laparotomy?

answerOptions.text: A) Less than 1%
D) Greater than 50%

questions.explanation: Umbilical adhesions after midline laparotomy exceed 50%, increasing entry risk.

questions.text: Why is umbilical entry riskier in obese patients?

answerOptions.text: A) It increases nerve injury risk
B) It limits visualization
C) The umbilicus shifts caudally
D) There is more fat in the peritoneal cavity

questions.explanation: With increasing BMI, the umbilicus migrates caudally, the wall thickens, and a 90° entry angle is often preferred to reduce preperitoneal tracking—though depth control is critical.

questions.text: Which trocar size always requires fascial closure?

answerOptions.text: A) 5 mm
B) 8 mm
C) 10 mm dilating
D) 12 mm cutting

questions.explanation: Ports ≥12 mm—particularly cutting trocars—should be closed; some 10 mm sites may also merit closure based on location/use.

questions.text: What port site has the highest risk of hernia formation?

answerOptions.text: A) Left lower quadrant
B) Transverse suprapubic
C) Midline/periumbilical extraction site
D) Right upper quadrant

questions.explanation: Midline/periumbilical sites, especially used for extraction, have the highest hernia risk.

questions.text: Why do dilating trocars create smaller fascial defects than cutting trocars?

answerOptions.text: A) They stretch rather than cut the tissue
B) They cauterize tissue as they enter
C) They are shorter in length
D) They only enter subcutaneously

questions.explanation: Dilating tips expand/separate fibers instead of sharply cutting fascia, leaving smaller defects.

questions.text: The inferior epigastric vessels lie:

answerOptions.text: A) Medial to the medial umbilical ligament
B) Lateral to the medial umbilical ligament
C) Posterior to the bladder
D) Inferior to the round ligament

questions.explanation: Inferior epigastrics course lateral to the medial umbilical ligament on the posterior rectus surface.
questions.submodule: Module 6.6 – Safety and Lateral Port Placement

questions.text: Where should lateral ports be placed to avoid injury to the inferior epigastric vessels?

answerOptions.text: A) Anywhere along the midline
B) Within 4–8 cm of the midline
C) Within 4 cm or beyond 8 cm from the midline
D) Directly at the level of ASIS

questions.explanation: Staying either very medial (≤4 cm) or more lateral (≥8 cm) helps avoid the typical epigastric course.
questions.submodule: Module 6.6 – Safety and Lateral Port Placement

questions.text: Which of the following nerves is most commonly injured during lateral port placement?

answerOptions.text: A) Genitofemoral
B) Ilioinguinal
C) Obturator
D) Pudendal

questions.explanation: Ilioinguinal/iliohypogastric nerves are most at risk with lateral ports and can cause chronic pain if injured.
questions.submodule: Module 6.6 – Safety and Lateral Port Placement
